schools.summary: Augustine: the chapter commends Christ’s humanity — concealing himself he kept his power, offering an example to our weakness; the Head doing what he sanctions in the member.
Chrysostom: ‘had not power’ is no weakness (he cast the soldiers back); envy and malice, by which death entered the world, drive the Jews to murder.
Calvin: he confined himself within human limits, having emptied himself (Phil 2); conducted himself as a man liable to dangers.
Henry: not ‘durst not’ but ‘would not’ — prudence, not fear; gospel light is taken from those who would quench it.
‘After these things’ skips the ~6-month withdrawal (passover to tabernacles); imperfects picture itinerant ministry and settled hostility (5:18).

outliers.note: Reads the concealment as Christ deliberately modelling for his members the lawful flight from persecution he himself did not need.

schools.summary: Tabernacles (skēnopēgia, hapax) on 15 Tisri, ~5 months after Passover; booths recall the wilderness; a pilgrim and harvest feast Josephus calls the greatest; later branch/altar ceremonies set up v.37.

Calvin: the feast recalls God’s grace in the wilderness; Christ usually came to feasts (subject to the Law; gospel opportunity), yet now stays retired. Gill: it typifies Christ’s tabernacling in flesh.
The ‘brethren’ (half-brothers, Robertson; cousins/relations, others) challenge him with heavy irony to ‘manifest’ himself in Judea before the rulers and the earlier-made disciples (2:23; 4:1).
Calvin: they ridicule his concealment as folly — he ‘wishes to be something’ yet ‘wants confidence in himself.’

sides.position: half-brothers (sons of Joseph and Mary)
cousins / distant relations (Hebrew idiom)
Joseph’s children by a former wife (some of the ancients)

schools.summary: Sound in the abstract but evil in motive; the condition ‘if thou do these things’ assumes yet quietly doubts the works; they crave Messianic pomp and a ‘name’ at Jerusalem.
Calvin: they trample the proofs of his divine power; near relations, as Satan’s instruments, tempt God’s children to ambition and avarice.
The brethren’s plain unbelief after the Nazareth/Beelzebub rejections; yet later they pray with the Apostles (Acts 1:14), changed by the risen Lord’s appearing (1 Cor 15:7).
Calvin: carnal kinship is worthless without faith; the new creature, not blood, makes one Christ’s brother (Mt 12:50) — against Mary-veneration grounded on relationship.
‘Time’ = kairos, the fitting occasion for a public Messianic manifestation (not the hōra of death); he avoids going up with the crowds to shun parade and the rulers’ envy.
Calvin: the ‘time’ is of setting out for the feast; the world favours the brothers but is Christ’s mortal enemy, so he cannot move at their convenience.
The world cannot hate its own; it hates Christ because his testimony exposes its evil — light hated by those who love darkness (3:19); a ‘law of moral correspondence’ (Westcott).
Calvin: peace with the world is bought by consent to vice; the unregenerate are Christ’s adversaries because the gospel summons all as guilty (16:8).
Against Porphyry’s charge of falsehood: ‘I go not up’ (ouk, Robertson) is no contradiction of v.10, since Christ refuses only the open, caravan manner; he goes later, secretly, at mid-feast (v.14).

debates.question: Does ‘I go not up to this feast’ contradict his later going up (Porphyry’s charge)?

sides.position: no — he refuses only the open, caravan manner the brothers urged, not the going itself
‘ouk’ (not) is the harder, likely original reading
‘not yet’ (oupō), found in some MSS, eases the difficulty

schools.summary: Chrysostom/Theophylact: he abides, then goes up secretly ‘as it were’ — by economy — to teach us (who cannot check our persecutors) the way of life, not merely to prove the Incarnation.
Constative aorist ‘abode’ covers a few days; he stayed behind only three or four before reaching Jerusalem (v.14).
Calvin: the cousins keep an easy peace with unbelievers; ‘accursed be that peace’ that abandons the Son of God.

outliers.note: Reads Christ’s human conduct as deliberate moral instruction for disciples who lack his power to overawe their enemies.

themes: going up ‘as it were in secret’

schools.summary: He goes up ‘not openly but as it were in secret,’ pointedly reversing the brothers’ ‘manifest thyself openly’ (v.4); the secrecy is only of the journey, not his later public teaching; he appears at mid-feast (v.14).
‘The Jews’ = the hostile rulers (not the crowds); the imperfect ‘were seeking’ and the contemptuous ‘Where is that one?’ reveal a widely-known plot and an anticipated crisis.
Calvin: the one true Church appears as ‘a shapeless chaos,’ the people whispering under the tyranny of priests and scribes.

‘Multitudes’ (plural only here) murmur, split over his character — ‘a good man’ (agathos, pure in motive) vs ‘he deceiveth the people’ (planaō, the rulers’ future ‘deceiver’); slander had the rulers’ backing.
Calvin: division over Christ is no new evil — be armed with a shield against it, and beware the rashness of judging God’s things lightly.

debates.question: Is Jesus ‘a good man’ or one who ‘deceiveth the people’?

sides.position: a good man, pure in motive
a deceiver leading the people astray

schools.summary: None spoke ‘openly’ (boldly) for fear of the rulers — the same dread that silenced the disciples (19:38; 20:19); Barnes: many timid friends praise his life but not his deity or atonement.
Calvin: the rulers wished his name ‘buried in oblivion’ — gross tyranny; yet Christ’s power shone the brighter, heard amidst armed foes.
Mid-feast (Chol Moʿed) is, so far as known, Christ’s first public appearance as a teacher, likely expounding a read Scripture; he stands suddenly before the very leaders who sought him (v.11).
Chrysostom/Theophylact: he delays to heighten attention, appearing when the crowd, done with the feast’s ceremonies, is readier to hear him.
Augustine: on the charge that he ‘seduceth the people’ — to lead from evil to good is to be a good ‘seducer,’ the comfort of true preachers.

Calvin: he did not desist from his office for fear; ceremonies need doctrine to be ‘lively images,’ so Christ teaches where the priests are dumb. Henry: the gospel is one ‘to the plain and to the polite.’

outliers.note: Redeems the word ‘seducer’: leading men from evil to good is a good seduction — the office of every faithful preacher.

schools.summary: They marvel not at his teaching’s substance but that an untrained ‘layman’ commands the learning (grammata) of the rabbinic schools (Acts 4:13; 26:24) — ‘his learning, not his wisdom’ (Dods/Westcott).
Calvin: their wonder seeks occasion to despise him; we ignore the divine hand in ordinary means and, from pride, let the extraordinary ‘pass away as a dream.’
His doctrine is not self-originated but the Father’s who sent him — the language of an ambassador (Isa 11:2), a bold claim of a source superior to the rabbis’ (Talmud vs NT).
Calvin: the offence is a ladder — an untaught teacher proves he was ‘taught by God’; the Son came from a workshop, not the schools, that the gospel’s divine origin be manifest.

The stress is on ‘willeth’: a will to do God’s will is the condition of knowing the doctrine — experimental knowledge needing moral harmony (Westcott); the ‘any man’ excludes none (Saul, Cornelius, Justin).
Calvin: sound judgment flows from the fear of God; the hearers fail to tell truth from falsehood because they lack piety and the desire to obey.
To speak ‘of himself’ is to seek one’s own glory; Christ seeks the sender’s glory, so is ‘true’ and ‘no unrighteousness’ (= no falsehood/imposture, adikia vs alēthēs) is in him.
Calvin: the glory sought is the touchstone — what displays God’s glory is divine, what feeds human ambition is to be rejected; no teacher is faithful unless void of ambition.
Origen reasons from ‘no unrighteousness’ toward Christ as righteousness, since the righteous Lord orders all things righteously.

outliers.note: Turns ‘no unrighteousness in him’ into a meditation on Christ as righteousness itself, mirroring the righteous God.

themes: seeking God’s glory vs. one’s own
the mark of a true teacher

schools.summary: Those who prided in Moses’ law break it by seeking his life (Rom 3:10); ‘and yet’ (kai adversative) alludes to 5:18; the Law was read at this very (Sabbatical-year) feast (Deut 31:10).
Chrysostom: he answers both charges — to break a law to heal is a beneficial transgression; ‘you kill,’ which the law forbids, is for an evil end.
Calvin: he reasons from the person — their free indulgence in vice unmasks their ‘zeal for the Law’ as hypocrisy.
Augustine: Truth and Charity were speaking; Christ foresaw whom his word would profit — blessed are they who have not seen yet believe.
The ignorant Galilean pilgrims (not the plotting rulers) cry ‘thou hast a devil’ = ‘thou art mad,’ unaware of the plot (5:18); spoken in wonder, not hostility (Ellicott).
Calvin: ‘hast a devil’ was the idiom for madness; the crowd, ignorant of the priests’ designs, slanders Christ — a warning against opinions on what we do not understand.

sides.position: the ignorant pilgrim multitude (better text)
the hostile ‘Jews’ (a manuscript variant)

schools.summary: ‘One work’ = the Sabbath healing of ch.5 that provoked the plot (5:18); the marvel was the apparent Sabbath-breaking; some join ‘dia touto’ (because of this) to v.21.
Calvin: leaving persons for the fact, Christ notes he is blamed for one Sabbath work while they do many (circumcisions) on every Sabbath uncondemned.
Circumcision is ‘of the fathers’ (Abraham, Gen 17), only adopted by Moses (Lev 12:3); by rabbinic rule (Hillel) it ‘nulleth the sabbath’ when the eighth day falls on it.
Calvin: their own Sabbath circumcising sets up an argument from the greater to the less — the partial rite vs the whole healing of a man.

From lesser to greater: if circumcision (a partial work, removing ‘a small portion of flesh’) rightly overrides the Sabbath, how much more making ‘a whole man sound’; ‘are ye wroth’ (cholate) pictures bitter spleen.
Calvin: both circumcision and the cure are works of God, but Christ argues the greater from the less; their anger is prejudice against his person, not zeal for the Law.
Judge by substance not surface (kat’ opsin = superficially); the Sabbath law is subordinate to mercy; ‘judge righteous judgment’ is itself a Mosaic precept (Deut 1:16-17) read at this feast.
Calvin: their wrong judgment springs from prejudice against his person; right judgment must be ‘regulated by the truth of the fact,’ not appearances.
The Jerusalem residents (distinct from the pilgrims) know the rulers’ plot (5:18) and marvel that he speaks boldly unhindered.
Chrysostom: ‘them of Jerusalem,’ who saw the most miracles, are the most pitiable — surrendering judgment to corrupt rulers; the murderers’ sudden silence is itself a proof of his Godhead.
Augustine: the earlier ‘secret’ going signified Christ hidden in the feast whose mystery was his own; now supposed timidity shows itself as power.
Calvin/Henry: they wonder at the rulers’ restraint but miss the providence of God, who ties men’s hands though he does not turn their hearts.

From the rulers’ silence the townsmen infer a possible secret change of mind; the question (mēpote, ingressive ‘come to know’) expects ‘no’ and carries sarcasm; ‘very’ (alēthōs) is absent in the best MSS.

They claim to know his (Galilean, Joseph-and-Mary) origin against the notion that Messiah’s ‘whence’ is unknown; Lightfoot harmonises with v.42 — a Bethlehem birth, then hiddenness, then reappearance from an unknown place.
Calvin: their imagined knowledge of his origin is the veil over their eyes; Wesley: they would not follow even believing rulers, ‘because they knew whence he was.’
Calvin: ironic reproach — 'you know all things yet know nothing'; God is 'true'/faithful, so the gospel stands though the world rage.
They know the phenomenal, not the truly existent One who sent him; 'and yet I am not come of myself' exposes their ignorance of the Father.
Clarke: Christ takes them on their own profession — his mission is attested by Baptist, Spirit, voice, doctrine, miracles, but they know not God.

debates.question: Is 'ye know me, and know whence I am' ironic, a question, or a partial concession?

sides.position: bitter irony — they know nothing
a partial concession — they know the surface only

schools.summary: Christ's unique knowledge belongs to One only, grounded in his origin and mission; he is 'the Father's Apostle to men' (John 1:18; 17:25).
Calvin: a warrant for bold confidence in God's truth, yet the bare words are not a sufficient proof of eternal deity against the Arians.
Wesley: 'from him' = eternal generation, 'sent me' = the mission flowing from it, answering their two questions.

debates.question: Does 'I am from him' prove Christ's eternal divinity, or only his heavenly mission?

sides.position: it answers eternal generation + mission
it infers heavenly descent but is no decisive proof against Arians

schools.summary: Calvin: Christ was guarded by God's protection; his death was by the Father's decree, and the time of every man's death is fixed by God.
The imperfect marks repeated efforts; 'his hour' shows John's theme of a predestined course (2:4; 8:20; 13:1), the immediate restraint being the divided crowd.
Wesley: 'his hour' = the time of his suffering.
Chrysostom: not a sound faith — moved by miracles not doctrine; Augustine: the humble received the physician the rulers sought to kill.
The question rests on the Messianic expectation of many miracles (Isa 35:5-6); an advance from 'good man' (v.12) to 'many believed.'
Calvin: even this miracle-leaning, weak faith is honoured as a 'small spark' acceptable to God; Henry: convinced but lacking courage to confess.
Pharisees enlist the Sadducean chief priests in the Sanhedrin to send temple police; the crowd's sympathy (v.31) provoked them.
Calvin: rival parties conspire together under Satan against the Son of God — a shaming spur to our own zeal for Christ's kingdom.
Clarke: the spreading conviction that Jesus is Messiah is exactly what moves them to seek his death.
Barnes/Clarke: tender warning to persecutors to seek him while he remains — about six months before the cross.
'I go unto him that sent me' (hypagō, to withdraw to the Father) is enigmatic to the hearers; the end is now at hand.
Calvin: spoken to his enemies — he ridicules their futile efforts, reproaches their ingratitude, and warns grace will soon be withdrawn.
Clarke/Barnes: a national seeking — vainly looking for a deliverer in the Roman judgment, not individual salvation.
Calvin: the vain groans of the reprobate, like Esau — they 'seek' without really seeking; receive Christ while present.
Ellicott/Robertson: a general truth (cf. 8:21; 13:33) of his return to the Father and unapproachable presence, not the fall of Jerusalem.

debates.question: What is the fruitless 'seeking' of Christ?

sides.position: a national seeking of a deliverer in coming judgment
the reprobate's vain, impenitent groans
a general truth of his unapproachable ascended presence

themes: where I am ye cannot come

schools.summary: The scornful guess about the diaspora is an unconscious prophecy of the Gentile mission, which grew out of the synagogues (Acts 15:21).
Calvin: proof of their earth-bound stupidity — they hear 'the Father' and think only of distant lands; the threatening did not affect them.
Wesley leaves open both 'Jews scattered in Greece' and 'teach the heathen themselves.'
Their scorn gives place to genuine wonder; the unintelligible saying baffles even the rulers (and later Peter, 13:37).

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns another scene and does not bear on v.36.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: on the last/great day, when all were gathered, Christ cries the free invitation — no compulsion, but for those whose desire is strong.
Augustine: an inner thirst of the inner man — come not by feet but by affection and love; grounded in Matthew 5:6.
'Cried' marks intensity; the eighth 'great day' of Tabernacles is the setting; the imagery draws on John 4:14 and Isaiah 55:1.
Calvin: 'if any thirst' excludes the self-satisfied; the invitation is free, yet only the thirsty come.
Henry: a gospel invitation and promise, given on the last day to the gathered, departing crowd.

debates.question: Where does the sentence break — does 'as the Scripture said' attach to believing or to the rivers?

sides.position: stop after 'as the Scripture said'; 'rivers...' is Christ's own assertion
the Scripture is the source of the 'rivers' image (Prov 5:16 / prophets)

outliers.note: Links the cry to the Word who 'stands among you' as the sovereign principle permeating all creation.

schools.summary: Chrysostom/Cyril: 'belly' = the heart; 'rivers' (plural) = the overflowing, ever-working grace of the Spirit, seen in Stephen, Peter, Paul.
Augustine: the drinker becomes a fountain; the river flowing out is love of neighbour, which keeps the stream from drying up.
A free citation paralleling the 'well springing up' of John 4:14; the believer is filled so as to overflow to others.
Calvin: believers are not barren soil but water their neighbours from the abundance received.
Augustine: the Spirit existed and worked before, but the full giving waited on Christ's exaltation; Chrysostom: gifts wait on the cross, for friends not enemies.
'Not yet given' = not the absolute absence of the Spirit but the not-yet of the full Pentecostal outpouring.
Calvin: the Spirit was always in the Church, but the abundant manifest gift waited on Christ's glorification to apply the finished redemption.
Augustine: the word sifts the crowd — some speak after the manner of the multitude, others rightly own him; 'the Prophet' distinguished from 'the Christ.'
'The Prophet' (Deut 18:15) is a distinct expected figure; some now dare to confess Jesus as Messiah outright.
Calvin: the gospel occasions schism but is not its cause — the seed of division lay hidden in men and is only awakened by the word.
A bolder party openly confesses him Messiah; others object from his supposed Galilean origin (expecting 'No').
Calvin: the gospel occasions schism but is no reproach; their one error is making Christ a Galilean, which a little inquiry would have corrected.
Clarke: a little examination would have shown his birth fulfilled prophecy; men still perish by trusting 'common report.'
A true premise (Davidic seed, Bethlehem — Micah 5:2) wrongly applied in ignorance of the actual birth; John faithfully records the crowd's error.
Wesley: they could not have forgotten Bethlehem — Herod's slaughter gave terrible reason to remember it.
Cyril: God's oath to David (Ps 132:11) of an enduring throne is fulfilled in Christ's kingdom, not Solomon's.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the widow's mite and does not bear on v.42.

schools.summary: The word is 'schisma,' a clear rent; John uses it of the crowd's split (9:16; 10:19), a term that later entered the Church's history.
Gill: the threefold division fulfils Christ's own prediction; the schism witnesses to his word.
The imperfect 'were wishing to seize him' echoes v.30; restraint came from the sympathetic section and the power of his presence (18:6).
Clarke: their disunity (v.43) was, under God, why his life was preserved — 'the remainder of wrath thou wilt restrain' (Ps 76:10).
Calvin: their cruel eagerness failed by God's providence, his hour not yet come.
Chrysostom: the plotting Pharisees were harmed while the officers, subdued by one sermon, became bold heralds of Christ's wisdom.
Henry: the rulers, skipping worship to scheme, fret at their own officers' failure (Ps 112:10).
The temple police of v.32 return to the Sanhedrin empty-handed, themselves half-won to Christ.
Henry: a great truth — none spoke like Christ; the arresters were 'taken with him,' preserved by God's power over the conscience of bad men.
Chrysostom: they were won by the teaching alone, not signs — 'Never man spake thus'; impartial minds need no long arguments.
A candid confession from men sent to arrest him; the word's self-authenticating force disarmed them.
Henry: the Pharisees flatter the officers' vanity and brand the gospel a 'cheat' — the real deceived being those who looked for worldly dominion.
Calvin: priestly pride and self-love breed tyranny and contempt of others.
A rhetorical sneer expecting denial — assuming no one of sense could follow the Galilean.
Henry: the appeal to the example of the great — yet some rulers did believe (Nicodemus next), and men are swayed by rank in religion.
The boast was even then untrue; God chose the weak (1 Cor 1:26-27), and the despised crowd's faith would outlast the rulers' unbelief.
Calvin: the rulers measure truth by their own rank — pride that abuses others and blinds itself.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns another scene and does not bear on v.48.

schools.summary: The rabbinic scorn of the 'am hâ-âretz' ('people of the earth') against the law-instructed; yet the common people heard Jesus gladly (Mk 12:37).
Henry: an unjust reproach — unlearned disciples may know God's word better (Ps 119:99); to curse them usurps God's prerogative (Prov 26:2).
Calvin: the concealed motive was that they thought holiness lay only in their own rank — like the Popish claim to be the only Church.
A courageous protest from within the Sanhedrin; Nicodemus, the night visitor (3:2), answers their own 'have any rulers believed?'
Henry: he kept his seat not from cowardice but to serve Christ within, like Hushai or Daniel; later openly bold (19:39).
Calvin: a 'neutral man' with a spark of godliness yet excessive timidity, who cannot bear the truth oppressed.

debates.question: Was Nicodemus's keeping his council seat cowardice or strategy?

sides.position: a strategic position to serve Christ within
excessive timidity, only a spark of courage

schools.summary: Nicodemus appeals to the law's rule against condemning the unheard (Ex 23:1; Deut 1:16); the law's expounders were its breakers.
Henry: he prudently argues from their own law, waiving Christ's doctrine and miracles, and retorts their charge of legal ignorance.
Factually false — Jonah, Nahum, Hosea (and others) were Galilean; ridicule, not argument, and they cut off reply (2 Kings 14:25).
Wesley: they knew the contrary and that Jesus was born in Bethlehem; a poor answer that would not bear examination.
Calvin: their fury at the mild Nicodemus betrays their hatred of Christ — like enemies who brand any candour heresy.
Robertson/Ellicott: this verse begins the pericope adulterae (7:53–8:11), absent from the best MSS — a genuine-sounding story but not original to John.
Calvin: the self-dissolving of so formidable a conspiracy shows it was scattered by the hand of God; Barnes sees pride and anger in disorder.
Wesley: one plain question of Nicodemus broke up the council — a word spoken in season (Prov 15:23).

debates.question: Is 7:53 (opening the pericope adulterae) original to John's Gospel?

sides.position: not genuine to John, though likely a true story
treated as the historical close of the council
